subsection.title: 0-forms
subsection.p: A 0-form is a function: \boxed{ f. }
Examples: f(x), \qquad f(x,y), \qquad f(x,y,z).
A 0-form is integrated over signed points: \boxed{ \int_{+P}f=f(P), \qquad \int_{-P}f=-f(P). }
For an oriented interval, \boxed{ \partial[a,b]=\{b\}-\{a\}. }
So \boxed{ \int_{\partial[a,b]}f=f(b)-f(a). }

subsection.title: 1-forms
subsection.p: A 1-form in the plane has the form \boxed{ \omega=P(x,y)\,dx+Q(x,y)\,dy. }
A 1-form in space has the form \boxed{ \omega=P(x,y,z)\,dx+Q(x,y,z)\,dy+R(x,y,z)\,dz. }
It is integrated over oriented curves.
If \mathbf F=(P,Q,R), then the work 1-form is \boxed{ \omega_{\mathbf F}=P\,dx+Q\,dy+R\,dz. }
Line integral: \boxed{ \int_C \mathbf F\cdot d\mathbf r = \int_C \omega_{\mathbf F}. }

subsection.title: 2-forms in the plane
subsection.p: A 2-form in the plane has the form \boxed{ \alpha=f(x,y)\,dx\wedge dy. }
It is integrated over oriented plane regions.
\boxed{ \iint_R f\,dx\wedge dy } means signed accumulation of f over R.

subsection.title: 2-forms in space
subsection.p: A general 2-form in space can be written as \boxed{ \Phi = A\,dy\wedge dz + B\,dz\wedge dx + C\,dx\wedge dy. }
This ordering matches flux.
If \mathbf F=(P,Q,R), the flux 2-form is \boxed{ \Phi_{\mathbf F} = P\,dy\wedge dz + Q\,dz\wedge dx + R\,dx\wedge dy. }
Flux integral: \boxed{ \iint_S \mathbf F\cdot\widehat{\mathbf n}\,dS = \iint_S \Phi_{\mathbf F}. }

subsection.title: 3-forms
subsection.p: A 3-form in space has the form \boxed{ \Omega=f(x,y,z)\,dx\wedge dy\wedge dz. }
It is integrated over oriented solids.

subsection.title: Degree
subsection.p: The degree of a form is the number of differentials wedged together.

statement.p: A 1-form is not a vector field, though a vector field can produce a 1-form.
A 2-form is not a vector field, though a vector field can produce a flux 2-form.
Forms remember orientation. Reversing orientation changes the sign of the integral.
